name: 'jam dbyangs chos rje bkra shis dpal ldan
comments:
First throne holder of 'Bras spungs (ID: 6751) (1416-1449).
[BKUHJCNA]: 744. Las chen models his activities explicitly after Po to ba rin chen gsal (ID: 3829) (composing many textual commentaries), Spyan snga tshul khrims 'bar (ID: 3830) (erecting supports of body, speech, and mind), and Phu chung ba gzhon nu rgyal mtshan (ID: 7093) (making offerings to the Three Jewels).

[BKUHJCNA]: 745. At 'Bras spungs (ID: 6751), his disciples established bshad grwa of phar, tshad, and dbu.

alternative_names:

comments:
Eighth throne-holder of gSang phu gling smad and 16th mkhan po of the Gling smad.
It is reported that during 'Jam dbyang Śākya gzhon nu's time as abbot of the sGros seminary of the Lower monastery (ca. 1326), there was a split between the old (rnying) and the new (gsar). Śākya gzhon nu's oral teachings were taught in "Old sGros" ([7FZ3B926]: 118).
This, however, conflicts with the dates determined for the dates of activity of the next abbot in [CCSBSVCA]: 686, namely 1293-1301.

alternative_names: rtogs ldan 'jam dpal rgya mtsho
comments:
According to Las chen, he had a wish to sustain instructions related to Uttaratantra (ID:3749), but Tsong kha pa advised him: "Listen to teachings on tshad ma!" (tshad ma nyon). So he went to gSang phu and studied epistemology with Slob dpon dKon seng pa (ID:7350) ([BKUHJCNA]: 711).


According to Las chen, he is also renowned as Shug 'brum pa due to subsisting only on shug 'brum (juniper seeds?) for three years ([BKUHJCNA]: 714).

btsun pa ston gzhon
alternative_names:
ston pa gzhon nu
'jam dbyangs ston gzhon
comments:
Van der Kuijp ([FEHIEFEB]: 143) lists as potential candidates: sKag pa ston gzhon (whom he discards as this contemporary of Sa skya Paṇḍita would have been too old), sBas pa ston gzhon (ID: 6651) (a possible option, although he is not explicitly associated with epistemology), and Cog ro ston gzhon (as the most likely option).
Understood by Sun Weijin to be the "'jam dbyangs ston gzhon" in the Pramāṇavārttika lineage mentioned by 'Gos Lo tsā ba ([FEHIEFEB]: 143).
He traveled to the Mongol court in China in the early 1280s and translated in Tibetan Xuanzang's (ca. 602-64) Chinese version of Śaṅkarasvāmin's Nyāyapraveśa ([FEHIEFEB]: 165).
A "ston gzhon" - maybe the same person - is also listed as the author of two works in the [8H6M69W4]:
- Lam 'bras gyi bshad pa gsung ngag nyi ma'i 'od zer in 230 folios
- Kun gyi sna lam du ma grags pa'i rin po che'i gsung 'ga' <gnyan ston tshul rgyal dang ston gzhon gnyis kyis> zin bris su mdzad pa rnams in 38 folios. ([FEHIEFEB]: 166).

name: bya 'dul 'dzin brtson 'grus 'bar
alternative_names:
bya 'dul 'dzin
bya 'dul brtson 'grus 'bar
bya 'du ba 'dzin ba brtson 'grus 'bar
dge bshes zul phu ba
sangs rgyas bya 'dul 'dzin brtson 'grus 'bar
zul bya 'dul ba 'dzin pa
zul phul bya 'dul ba 'dzin pa chen po
comments:
Born in 1091 at gTsang rong gyung and died at the age of 76 in Zul phu according to [R894SIKE]: 109.
[W4T5ZMUP]: 38 accordingly proposes the dates 1091-1166.
[VKBZTBWN], fol. 3b6 reports that he was born the year rNgog lo (ID:3786) came back to Tibet, i.e., ca. 1092.
On his life, based on the Zul phu mkhan byang chub rin chen dpal bzang po'i rnam par thar pa (ID:6715), see [W4T5ZMUP]: 40.
It is unclear whether he is the same person as rGya 'dul ba 'dzin pa (ID:4193). The [NA9AMDH6] distinguishes two "rGya 'dul 'dzin" - the elder (ID: 3941) and the younger (ID:4193) - and a "Bya 'dul 'dzin brTson 'grus 'bar" (student of 'Bre chen po). In contrast, the [R894SIKE] only mentions one "rGya 'dul 'dzin" (the elder), distinct from "Bya 'dul 'dzin" (who was ordained by 'Bre).
[VKBZTBWN], fol. 6b5-6 and [8VLVHDR2], fol. 14b5 report that he was ordained by 'Bre and received from him the name in religion brTson 'grus 'bar.
A number of the same teachers are mentioned for rGya 'dul 'dzin the younger and Bya 'dul 'dzin but, for instance, the latter's studies with rGya 'dul 'dzin the older took place when he was a retired abbot, whereas the former's studies with him took place when he was still abbot.
Further, rGya 'dul 'dzin the younger became abbot of dGa' ba sdong and Bya 'dul abbot of Zul phu.
This suggests they might have been distinct persons.

comments:
Fl. late 16th early 17th c.
Identification and dates following [CCSBSVCA]: 688, n. 18. 
Reconverted Zul phu seminar into a dGe lugs site in ca. 1639 (see [W4T5ZMUP]: 39). 
[W4T5ZMUP]: 36, n. 47 identifies him as the seat-holder of sKyor mo lung from 1618 to 1644, seat-holder of gSang phu from 1619, and seat-holder of dGa' ldan between 1626 and 1637.
[CCSBSVCA]: 688, n. 18. Mentions his tenure of sKyor mo lung in 1618, gSang phu in 1619, rDzing phyi in 1620, gSang sngags mkhar in 1621, dGa' ldan in 1625.

name: dge 'dun grub pa
alternative_names: tA la'i bla ma 01
comments:
[BKUHJCNA]: 703. Las chen reports that in dGe 'dun grub's own words, he "took notes on [Tsong kha pa's] teaching on the difficult points of valid cognition (tshad ma'i dka' gnas gsungs pa la zin bris kyang mdzad)." However, such a work does not appear extant in either figure's Collected Works. In a possible reference to this same work, Las chen relates on p. 757: tshad ma rnam nges chos mchog gi 'grel pa'i steng nas dka' gnad gcod pa gsan te zin bris kyang mdzad.

[BKUHJCNA]: 756. He studied Pramāṇavārttikakārikā (ID: 3772) with one Yongs su 'dzin pa A bha ya kIrti and performed grwa bskor at Snar thang (ID: 4411)...later, he served as an attendant to Sems dpal chen po kun dga' bzang po (ID: 7089) and heard teachings on tshad ma that caused his aspirations (thugs bskyed) to blossom, leading him to exclaim: "This is the first time someone has made me really understand it!" (dang po rang nas rig chen byas).

comments:
First Karmapa.
His birth name was dGe 'phel ([Y6NGTTC6]: 24). His alternative names and epithets are listed in [Y6NGTTC6]: 5 and 27, which also lists the main information on his life found in early sources.
Dus gsum mkhyen pa (ID:3855), Sho sgom (ID:3857), and Phag mo gru pa (ID:3856) are known as the "grub thob mi gsum" and as the "khams pa mi gsum." According to Dus gsum mkhyen pa'i rnam thar in Si tu Chos kyi 'byung gnas's Karma kam tshang gser 'phreng (cited in [I58Z22ET]: 408, n. 42), Dus gsum mkhyen became known as one of the "khams pa mi gsum" during his stays in gSang phu (ID:4332) (gsang phur yang bzhugs te | khams pa mi gsum du grags |).

name: gling smad pa shes rab dpal ldan
comments:
Fl. mid-15th c. 
He was abbot of gSang phu gling smad (ID:6158) when the Blue Annals were composed ([R894SIKE]: da lta'i shes rab dpal ldan pa 'di'o). But this does not seem to entail he was necessarily still the abbot when the work was finished in 1476, as the Blue Annals were composed over the course of many years.
He founded the Sa skya pa monastery of Rwa ba smad (ID:7085) ([CCSBSVCA]: 688, n. 11).

Credited with a commentary on the Tshad ma rigs gter (ID:5564) ([MKQ6FJV7]: 61, n. 7).

name: gnyag
comments:
The identity of "gNyag" (maybe a misspelling of gNyags) remains unknown.
It is suggested in [Z5WBK2VV]: 63 that he might be gNyags grags tshul, the abbot of bDe ba can in the teaching lineage of rGya 'ching ru ba (ID:4202).
[VSENG5SZ] identifies the author (apparently on the basis of the name alone) as gNyags chung Lo tsA ba (1013-1070) also named gNyag yon tan snying po, one of the four men who re-introduced monastic rules in Tibet. 
There are other candidates with the name "gNyag" or "gNyags", but their views on epistemology cannot be compared with those of the present author. Comparison remains to be carried out with the views ascribed to gNyags ye shes 'bar (ID:4264) by several 12th-century authors.

The closing verses mention 'Jam dbyangs 'od zer mgon po (ID:3893) as the source of the teaching - he could be 'Jam dbyangs shakya gzhon nu (ID:3894), the eighth abbot of the Lower monastery of gSang phu and founder of Tshal Gung thang. See [J4L5X9AG]: 432, n. 58.

comments:
Jo nang pa scholar. After studying with Dol po pa (ID:4213) he went back to Mi nyag and built the monastery of Nges don gyi chos gzhi dgon sde.  ([UNV7NRZ8]: 84).

It is not certain whether he is the same person as the "blo gros grags pa" mentioned in the biography of rMa se ston pa Rin chen bzang po (ID:6363), another student of Dol po po who founded rA tI monastery (this is plausible, as Blo gros grags pa was a student of Ye shes rdo rje, who was a student of rMa se ston pa).

name: mkhas grub rje dge legs dpal bzang
comments:
According to Las chen, one time at Byang ngam ring, mKhan chen Phyogs rgyal ba was refuting Rig gter (ID: 5564) and no one was able to give an answer, but mKhas grub overwhelmed him with his arguments based on scriptures and reasoning ([BKUHJCNA]: 732).

According to PaN chen, he became renowned as mKhas grub chos rje in all regions due to being the rebirth (sku'i skye ba) of mKhas grub Lha dbang blo or Devendrabuddhi (ID: 5554) ([E9XCQDGQ]: 99).

name: mnyes thang pa blo gros rgya mtsho (2)
comments:
Fl. mid 17th c.
May be the same person as (ID: 6681).
This person is recorded by Sangs rgyas rgya mtsho in his Vaidūrya gser po as the 48th abbot of the upper college of gSang phu monastery. It is unclear whether this figure is the same as (ID: 6383). See [MPRU5QUC]: 212.
[CCSBSVCA]: 688, n. 19 suggests he could be the chos rje lHa rje Blo bzang rgya mtsho active in the 1660s, rather than (ID:6383) holding a second term as abbot.

name: nor bzang rgya mtsho
alternative_names: mkhas grub nor bzang rgya mtsho
comments: Born in rTa nag in gTsang. Received training at bKra shis lhun po from dGe 'dun rgya mtsho. Gained a reputation at gSang phu, and arrived at dGa' ldan at 35. See [MKF6XDW8]: 85, n. 208.

alternative_names:

comments:
His name is found in the list of the "Eight Great Lion."
Maybe the same person as "Nyang bran chos kyi ye shes" (ID:3876), as suggested in [CCSBSVCA]: 164, n. 416. However, A khu ching distinguishes the two.
It is questionable whether he is the "chos kyi seng ge" named as author in the colophon of work (ID:3727) - this could also refer to Phya pa Chos kyi seng ge (ID:3831). Conversely, he could be a candidate for the authorship of work (ID:6554) whose author is also named as "chos kyi seng ge" in the colophon.

comments:

Author of manuals on the Śīkṣāsamuccaya (ID:3742) - see (ID: 6558) - and Bodhicaryāvatāra (ID: 3727). See [R894SIKE]: 405 (zhang tshe spong ba chos kyi bla mas kyang TI ka 'ga' re brtsams shing de'i slob ma nyan bran pa chos kyi ye shes kyis ni bslabs pa kun las btus pa dang | byang chub sems dpa'i spyod pa la 'jug pa la sogs pa spyod phyogs kyi yig cha dang |) and [EG6VZ8JK]: 26, translated from [8GL7ZT7Y]: 15b2 (chos kyi ye shes kyis bslab spyod sogs byang chub sems dpa’i sde sogs la bshad nyan yun ring zhing rnam bshad mang po mdzad ).

comments:
On his birth in 1109, the year of rNgog Blo ldan shes rab (ID:3786)'s passing, see [TM8DW8WA]: 32-33, [VKBZTBWN]: 4b4-5, and [8GL7ZT7Y]: 15b2-3.
On the date of his passing in a "sa mo glang" year, see [R894SIKE]: 400, [VKBZTBWN]: 4b4-5 and 6a5. On the 23rd of the third month, see [95UZA9Y5]: 40/3/5.
The majority of the information available on Phya pa has been provided in [EZ5JXHJY] and [8S7FDMCJ]. Summaries of the same are also found in [LQZU66YX]: 37-40, [F4UK2S8P]: 2-3, and [BW9UR427]: 35-36 (in French).
A list of his students is provided in [EZ5JXHJY]: 356, based on [MQB8JGAX]: 297/4, [KL9XTQWI]: I, 454, [4YEPFBIB]: 340-341, [8GL7ZT7Y], fol. 15b3, [VKBZTBWN], fol. 4b7-5a4.
[FDEWZ2NZ]: 290 also mentions the list of the four "jo sras" from bSod nams dpal's Bla ma rngog pa yab sras rim par byon pa’i rnam thar rin po che’i rgyan gyi phreng ba.

name: rgya 'dul 'dzin brtson 'grus 'bar
comments:
Also referred to as "rGya 'Dul 'dzin the younger" to distinguish him from "rGya 'Dul 'dzin the older" (ID:3941). See [647FFPZR]: 541.
It is unclear whether he is the same person as Bya 'dul ba 'dzin pa (ID:3818). The [NA9AMDH6] distinguishes two "rGya 'dul 'dzin" - the elder (ID: 3941) and the younger (ID:4193) - and a "Bya 'dul 'dzin brTson 'grus 'bar" (student of 'Bre chen po). In contrast, the [MQB8JGAX] only mentions one "rGya 'dul 'dzin" (the elder), distinct from "Bya 'dul 'dzin" (who was ordained by 'Bre). 
A number of the same teachers are mentioned for rGya 'dul 'dzin the younger and Bya 'dul 'dzin but, for instance, the latter's studies with rGya 'dul 'dzin the older took place when he was a retired abbot, whereas the former's studies with him took place when he was still abbot.
Further, rGya 'dul 'dzin the younger became abbot of dGa' ba sdong and Bya 'dul abbot of Zul phu.
This suggests they might have been distinct persons.

The author of an early Tibetan indigenous commentary on the Nyāyabindu (ID: 7165), whose first chapter is preserved in Tangut translation. The author's name appears only in Tangut in the colophon as "Źjɨr ɣwie." Because the first word, źjɨr, means "insight" and the second, "ɣwie," means "victory," the name can possibly be reconstructed as Shes rab rgyal ba. 
Many features of the commentary, such as the hermeneutic tools utilized and the persons cited in there, indicate the author was part of the intellectual tradition stemming from rNgog Blo ldan shes rab (ID:3786). 
The author quotes from early followers of rNgog Lo, such as Khyung Rin chen grags (ID:3809). However, in the extant part of the commentary, we do not see him quoting from Phya pa (ID:3831) or anyone after him. This suggests he was likely a contemporary of Phya pa. 
Also, a certain "Sher rgyal" documented in the Red Annals fits with Źjɨr ɣwie's intellectual and biographical profiles. Hence, they could be the same person. 

 For details, see [3HXACZSD]: 208–216.
